data.content_ref: inline:{"claim": "Harness strictly beats self-consistency only when the critic's recall on incorrect-majority questions exceeds a threshold that depends on base error e and k: R_wrong > (1 - P_right) / P_wrong, where P_wrong is the probability of an incorrect strict majority. When this inequality fails, false flags on correct majorities erode more right answers than true flags rescue, producing net loss or tie.", "mechanism": "Decompose the candidate pool by majority outcome. Let p = P(correct strict majority), q = P(incorrect strict majority), r = P(no strict majority), p+q+r=1. SC accuracy = p + r * tie_break_rate. Harness changes outcomes only on flagged questions: on correct-majority questions, a false flag (prob 1-phi_correct) may break the majority and lose the answer; on incorrect-majority questions, a true flag (recall R_wrong) may break the wrong majority and allow a correct minority to win (gain). Net = q * R_wrong * P(correct wins post-filter | wrong majority flagged) - p * (1 - phi_correct) * P(wrong wins post-filter | correct majority flagged). Harness strictly beats SC iff this net > 0. The threshold R_wrong > [(1-phi_correct) * p] / [q * P(correct wins post-filter)] emerges. In measured data: hard-set v4-pro has q ~ 0 (base error 0.01), so net ~ 0 regardless of R_wrong. Easy-set v4-flash has q ~ 0.04 and precision 0.333, meaning false flags dominate, net ~ 0 or slightly negative.", "scope": {"covers": ["regimes where incorrect-majority probability q is non-negligible (e in [0.10, 0.45] for k=5)", "k=5 (extensible to k=3,7)", "critic precision on correct-majority questions (phi_correct) >= 0.5"]}, "excludes": ["e < 1/(k+1) where q ~ 0", "e > 0.5 where correct minority post-filter probability vanishes", "critic that flags independently of correctness"]}, "forbidden": [{"case": "Experiment shows harness > SC by >= 0.05 at R_wrong = 0.3 and phi_correct = 0.5 with q = 0.15 and p = 0.65, refuting the threshold R_wrong > (1-phi_correct)*p / (q * P(correct wins post-filter))", "eval": "rubric:std-crit-design"}, {"case": "Experiment shows harness < SC by >= 0.03 at R_wrong = 0.8 and phi_correct = 0.9 with q = 0.2, refuting that high recall with high precision is sufficient", "eval": "rubric:std-crit-design"}, {"case": "Experiment shows the net harness-minus-SC is independent of R_wrong across all tested regimes, refuting the mechanism that recall on wrong majorities drives the gain", "eval": "rubric:std-crit-design"}], "prose_notes": "This conjecture provides a threshold-based refinement of 862de34b, making the necessary condition quantitative. It predicts that the hard-set v4-pro data (net 0) is explained by q ~ 0, not by critic quality, and that increasing e to ~0.15 with R_wrong > 0.6 should produce strict harness advantage. The experiment: 2 base-error tiers (e ~ 0.15 via medium-difficulty questions, e ~ 0.35 via hard questions) x 20 questions each x k=5 candidates x 2 arms (SC, harness) x 2 critic-prompt variants (strict vs lenient) = 40 questions x 5 x 2 x 2 = 800 model calls + 160 critic calls ~ 960 calls x ~300 tokens avg = ~290k tokens. Under budget."}
